metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a numeric floor (e.g. recall ≥ 0.90, false-acceptance ≤ 0.05) for the first run, or explicitly document that the initial run establishes the baseline and subsequent runs must meet or exceed it.
evidence: 'Packed recall may not decrease, false acceptance may not increase, coverage must be complete, and attribution errors must be zero.'
No initial/floor numeric values are stated anywhere in the plan.
Coverage=complete and attribution=zero are absolute and measurable; recall and false-acceptance are purely relative with no stated anchor.
findings.finding: The recall and false-acceptance thresholds for the opt-in prerequisite evaluation are stated relatively ('may not decrease', 'may not increase') but no baseline is pinned. On a first-ever run of this new spot eval there is no prior result to compare against, leaving the executor unable to determine pass/fail without additional clarification.
findings.location: Implementation — last bullet ('Add eight or more gold sets… Packed recall may not decrease, false acceptance may not increase'); AC: 'The opt-in prerequisite evaluation meets recall, false-acceptance, coverage, and attribution thresholds.'
scenarios: First execution of `--prerequisite-packing` has no prior result; the executor cannot determine whether exit 0 should be issued.
A partial implementation that achieves, e.g., 60% recall on the first run silently passes the relative threshold because there is nothing lower to compare against.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Packed recall may not decrease, false acceptance may not increase' with explicit first-run floors, e.g. 'recall ≥ 0.90, false-acceptance ≤ 0.10 on every run; baseline is set by the first green execution and must not regress thereafter.' Record the baseline values in the ticket comment alongside the spot-eval output.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider whether a thin vertical slice (e.g., implement and spot-eval the focused finder prompt alone, without the verifier, decide wiring, or schema extensions) could de-risk prompt quality before committing to full stack implementation — or explicitly justify why the full stack is the minimum viable increment.
evidence: The Implementation section sequences finder → verifier → decide → orchestrator → parity → spot-eval as a single deliverable
The spot-eval is the only quality gate for prompt quality, and it appears at the end rather than as an early evidence gate
'Add eight or more gold sets of at least three prerequisites' — the gold set design is specified upfront rather than emerging from a discovery phase
ABSENCE: no intermediate milestone that could be shipped/validated before wiring the full stack
findings.finding: The plan implements the full prerequisite-consistency stack (finder, verifier, decide integration, orchestrator verdict change, schema extensions, parity fields, and 8+ spot-eval gold sets) as a single big-bang leaf without a thin vertical slice that de-risks the riskiest unknown — LLM prompt quality for prerequisite consistency judgment — before committing to the full wiring.
findings.location: Implementation section (all sub-bullets) and Verification section
scenarios: If LLM prompt quality for prerequisite consistency is lower than expected, the entire stack must be revisited rather than discovering this early through a focused finder-only evaluation
The ticket takes significantly longer than one session because prompt iteration and full wiring are bundled

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `prerequisite_fidelity_report`'s return type (either a `ParityReport` or a new named type), its parameter list, and the exact formulas for recall, false-acceptance, coverage completeness, and attribution-error rate it computes.
evidence: Plan says: 'Add prerequisite_fidelity_report for authoritative-ID attribution plus recall and false acceptance'
The existing container_fidelity_report returns a ParityReport with specific metrics keys; no equivalent output schema is specified for prerequisite_fidelity_report

Plan says evaluation must pass 'Packed recall may not decrease, false acceptance may not increase, coverage must be complete, and attribution errors must be zero' — but the metric computation formula is not defined
The verification command references 'printed recall, false-acceptance, coverage, and attribution summary' but the fields of that summary are unspecified
findings.finding: The plan references `prerequisite_fidelity_report` as a new function providing 'authoritative-ID attribution plus recall and false acceptance' for the opt-in evaluation, but never defines its output structure, parameter types, or how it differs from the existing `container_fidelity_report`. The existing `ItemRecord` is extended with `gold_prerequisite_id` and `pred_prerequisite_id` fields, but the plan does not specify what `prerequisite_fidelity_report` returns (a `ParityReport`? a new type?) or how it aggregates attribution errors across the eight gold sets.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Add prerequisite_fidelity_report for authoritative-ID attribution plus recall and false acceptance'
scenarios: Implementor produces a function that prints metrics but does not enforce the thresholds, missing the gate that must enforce 'attribution errors must be zero'
The function returns a non-ParityReport type causing the CLI entry point to fail on exit-code determination
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: `prerequisite_fidelity_report(gold: Sequence[ItemRecord], pred: Sequence[ItemRecord]) -> ParityReport` reusing `parity_report` for recall/false-accept, and adding an attribution-error rate check (zero tolerance) analogous to `container_fidelity_report`'s attribution_accuracy floor.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the prerequisite-specific sizing adapter's function name, parameter list (at minimum: system_prompt_tokens, subject_tokens, prerequisite_block_tokens, output_reserve, headroom), return type, and the fit-test formula it uses to determine whether a bin fits the target window.
evidence: Plan says: 'Size the actual focused prompt topology, including system prompt, subject plan, prerequisite or finding blocks, output reserve, and headroom, through a prerequisite-specific adapter in sizing.py'
No function name, signature, or formula is given for the adapter

The halving/escalation fallback references 'Largest-window exhaustion emits indeterminate/evaluation-error with detail=input-too-large' but the test for 'largest-window exhaustion' requires a budget comparison the adapter must implement
Existing sizing.py adapters (verify_request_chunks, pass1_with_ladder) have precisely documented math; this adapter has none
findings.finding: The plan says to 'size the actual focused prompt topology... through a prerequisite-specific adapter in sizing.py' but provides no specification of this adapter's interface, parameters, or token-budget formula. The existing `sizing.py` provides `verify_request_chunks`, `pass1_with_ladder`, etc., each with documented token-budget math. The prerequisite-specific adapter is referenced only by effect ('on context-limit error, split a stable bin into contiguous halves recursively') but its function name, inputs (system prompt tokens, subject plan tokens, prerequisite block tokens, output reserve, headroom), and return type are never specified.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Size the actual focused prompt topology... through a prerequisite-specific adapter in sizing.py'
scenarios: Implementor creates an adapter without a headroom parameter, causing focused prompts to exceed context windows without triggering the halving fallback
The adapter's definition of 'bin fits in window' differs from the actual prompt assembly, causing asymmetric context-limit errors that bypass the halving logic

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate with the `_build_verification_output` extension spec the exact algorithm by which `plan_review_decide`'s focused reshape extracts `prerequisite_attribution_valid` from the verif binary dict and applies the indeterminate/attribution-invalid conversion.
evidence: Plan says: 'Extend _build_verification_output with an optional extra-binary-fields argument and have only plan_review_verification_model pass prerequisite_attribution_valid with default na'
Plan says: 'Do not add the field to shared _build_binary, _BINARY_NA_DEFAULT, or GRADED_BINARY'
Existing _build_binary in verify_models.py builds Binary from GRADED_BINARY + _BINARY_NA_DEFAULT exclusively; no extension point for extra fields exists

The veto rule ('no or na attribution converts that prerequisite record to indeterminate/attribution-invalid') is specified only in the plan_review_decide section, not adjacent to the schema definition
plan_review_decide section never references how it extracts prerequisite_attribution_valid from the verif binary dict at runtime
findings.finding: The `prerequisite_attribution_valid` field is added to `plan_review_verification_model` via an 'optional extra-binary-fields argument' to `_build_verification_output`, but the plan does not specify the concrete extension mechanism: whether `_build_binary` is called with an additional field list, whether a second Binary subclass is created, or whether `create_model` is called with an extra kwarg. The processing protocol — how Pass-3 in `plan_review_decide`'s focused domain reads this field out of the binary dict to apply the attribution veto — is described only in the decide section, not co-located with the schema extension in `verify_models.py`.
findings.location: Implementation section: '_build_verification_output with an optional extra-binary-fields argument' and 'plan_review_decide' focused domain reshape
scenarios: An implementor extending _build_verification_output creates the extra field correctly but puts the veto check in the wrong layer (kernel pass3_decide instead of plan_review_decide), causing attribution failures to silently affect general findings
The Binary model is built with the extra field but no code in plan_review_decide actually reads it from verif.get('binary', {}).get('prerequisite_attribution_valid'), leaving the veto inoperative

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `prerequisite_id` is added as a key to the focused finding dict before `partition_findings` (so `mint_finding_id` reads `finding.get('prerequisite_id')`) or whether `mint_finding_id` gains a keyword parameter, and identify all call sites that must be updated.
evidence: Plan says: 'Extend mint_finding_id so its hash basis appends |prereq:<canonical-id> when prerequisite_id is present; identical text from different prerequisites cannot deduplicate each other'
Existing mint_finding_id (orchestrator.py:328-333) takes only `finding: dict[str, Any]` with no prerequisite_id parameter

The plan says 'Concatenate general_decided + focused_decided only after both decide calls return, then invoke the existing single partition_findings call with DET findings' — meaning mint_finding_id is called inside partition_findings for all findings
No specification of whether focused findings carry prerequisite_id as a dict key before reaching partition_findings/mint_finding_id
findings.finding: `mint_finding_id` is extended to append `|prereq:<canonical-id>` when `prerequisite_id` is present, but the plan does not specify whether `prerequisite_id` is a field already present in the finding dict (added upstream when the finding is created) or a separate parameter added to `mint_finding_id`'s signature. The existing function takes only `finding: dict[str, Any]` and all callers in `partition_findings` pass a single dict. If the signature does not change, all call sites that call `mint_finding_id` inside `partition_findings` for focused findings must pre-populate `prerequisite_id` in the dict; if the signature changes, all general call sites need updating.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Extend mint_finding_id so its hash basis appends |prereq:<canonical-id> when prerequisite_id is present'
scenarios: Implementor adds prerequisite_id as a finding dict key during focused domain flattening but partition_findings' mint_finding_id call doesn't check for it, leaving the IDs identical for same-text findings from different prerequisites
Implementor changes mint_finding_id's signature to accept prerequisite_id=None breaking the 3 existing call sites in partition_findings
findings.suggested_fix: The simplest approach: specify that focused findings carry `prerequisite_id` as a standard dict key populated during focused domain flattening; `mint_finding_id` reads `finding.get('prerequisite_id')` with no signature change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one acceptance criterion (or extend `test_plan_review_gate.py` as a live-model integration test) that exercises the full prerequisite finder→verifier→decide→verdict path against a real LLM dependency, and map it to a concrete proving command in the Verification section.
evidence: 'Run .venv/bin/python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing' — described as testing prerequisite packing specifically, not full pipeline integration.
'Run .venv/bin/python -m pytest -m "not integration"' — excludes integration tests, meaning workflow tests run against mocked LLM.
The plan does not name any end-to-end or live-model proving command for the combined finder+verifier+decide+verdict flow.
E6(f) anti-FP for opt-in flag does not apply: the criterion is registered unconditionally in the production runner when prerequisites exist.
findings.finding: The new prerequisite-criterion code path — registered in the production Pass-1 batch runner and making real LLM calls — has no acceptance criterion that exercises it end-to-end against a live model. The fidelity spot eval (`--prerequisite-packing`) tests packing/batching correctness only; the workflow tests in `test_plan_review_workflow.py` are in the `pytest -m 'not integration'` suite and therefore use mocked/offline LLM responses. No proving command verifies that the full finder→verifier→decide→verdict pipeline executes correctly against an actual model dependency.
findings.location: Verification section; Implementation — 'Extend the production Pass-1 batch runner with the prerequisite-only finder route'; AC: 'Every upheld focused finding appears in the verdict with its authoritative prerequisite ID.'
scenarios: Mocked workflow tests pass (LLM stubs return well-formed outputs), but the real model returns a schema-invalid response; the pipeline silently degrades to indeterminate with no test catching it.
The focused verifier prompt is malformed in production but the mock returns a valid fixture — the defect ships undetected.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an integration-tagged test in `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_gate.py` (already in the non-`-m not integration` run) that submits a real plan with a synthetic pinned prerequisite to the full gate and asserts the returned verdict contains a `prerequisite_coverage` entry with the expected canonical ID. Reference it in an AC: 'End-to-end gate run with a live model returns a verdict whose prerequisite_coverage contains exactly one record per supplied prerequisite.' Run it via the existing `pytest tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_gate.py` command already listed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md section capturing the three key architectural decisions: (1) the `prerequisite_indeterminate`-before-`blocking` ordering in `finalize_verdict`, (2) the non-overlapping two-domain index invariant, and (3) the `attribution-invalid`→`indeterminate` conversion rationale.
evidence: No ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc entry is mentioned anywhere in the plan
'This story explicitly modifies `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`' — a certified epic requirement drives an ordering constraint that will be invisible to future editors without a doc trail
The two-domain index scheme ('Preserve two non-overlapping verification index domains') is a non-obvious invariant with no recorded rationale
findings.finding: The plan introduces several novel architectural decisions (two-domain index architecture, new focused Pass-1/Pass-2 LLM topology, `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` control-flow change, `mint_finding_id` hashing extension with prerequisite namespacing, new `PrerequisiteCoverageOutput` contract, and isolation invariants) with no mention of capturing them in an ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design document. Future maintainers modifying `passes.py`, `orchestrator`, or the verify pipeline have no authoritative record of why the focused domain is kept separate, why attribution failures convert to `indeterminate` rather than dropping findings, or why `prerequisite_indeterminate` is checked before `blocking`.
findings.location: Implementation section (overall); `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` paragraph; `plan_review_decide` paragraph; Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: A future author modifying `finalize_verdict` reorders the `if coverage.get('prerequisite_indeterminate')` check without knowing the certified epic ordering constraint
A contributor extending the verification pipeline merges the two index domains after misreading the intent
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0XXX-prerequisite-consistency-review.md` (or a named section in AGENTS.md) documenting these three decisions with their justifications before merge.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability (at minimum a structured log entry and ideally a metric counter) for focused-finder and focused-verifier calls covering call outcome (success / context-error / provider-error), latency, and token usage so operators can distinguish new-pass failures from general-pass failures.
evidence: Plan specifies error dispositions (indeterminate/evaluation-error) but contains no mention of logging, metrics, tracing, or alerting for the two new pass types.
No 'instrument' or 'observe' language anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The new focused-finder and focused-verifier LLM calls have no stated observability instrumentation (metrics, logs, or traces), so operators cannot distinguish elevated error rates, latency spikes, or token overruns on the new prerequisite pass from general-pass problems.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Extend the production Pass-1 batch runner with the prerequisite-only finder route' and focused verifier steps
scenarios: A provider starts returning errors only for prerequisite calls; operators see no signal until reviews silently emit INDETERMINATE verdicts.
Latency regression on focused calls goes undetected because no per-pass latency metric is emitted.
findings.suggested_fix: In `passes.py`, after each focused finder/verifier call site, emit a structured log record (e.g., `logger.info('prerequisite_pass', extra={'pass': PASS_PREREQUISITE_FINDER, 'outcome': ..., 'latency_ms': ..., 'tokens': ...})`) and increment a counter metric keyed by pass name and outcome.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the focused finder and verifier calls inherit or define their own retry/backoff policy, and document the number of attempts before the terminal indeterminate/evaluation-error disposition is emitted.
evidence: 'Non-context provider failure emits that record for every unresolved ID' — no mention of retry attempts before emitting indeterminate.
General-pass retry behaviour (if any) is not stated as being inherited by the focused paths.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify retry/backoff behaviour for transient provider errors on the new focused-finder and focused-verifier calls; it only specifies the terminal disposition (indeterminate/evaluation-error) but not whether a retry is attempted before giving up.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Non-context provider failure emits that record for every unresolved ID'
scenarios: A brief provider hiccup causes every prerequisite in a batch to be marked indeterminate/evaluation-error and the whole review to become INDETERMINATE, even though a single retry would have succeeded.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the `PlanRelationSnapshot` preload lifecycle: (a) confirm it is scoped per-review-run (not shared across concurrent calls), (b) define what 'preload succeeded' means when only some prerequisites are readable vs. when the preload itself errors, and (c) state how partial availability is surfaced to the focused criterion.
evidence: 'runs only when the shared PlanRelationSnapshot preload succeeded' — binary success/fail, no partial-failure handling.
'one relation preload supplies both pin creation and prerequisite routing' — ownership and retirement not stated.
No description of snapshot scoping (per-request vs. cached/shared).
findings.finding: The `PlanRelationSnapshot` preload lifecycle is underspecified: the plan does not state when the snapshot is created relative to the review run, whether it is shared across concurrent reviews of the same ticket, or how a partially-failed preload (some prerequisites readable, some not due to a transient error) is distinguished from a fully-unavailable preload.
findings.location: Upstream contract section and Implementation — 'runs only when the shared PlanRelationSnapshot preload succeeded'
scenarios: Two concurrent reviews for the same ticket share a preload object; one mutates or invalidates it mid-flight.
Preload succeeds for 3 of 5 prerequisites (network timeout on 2); the plan's binary 'succeeded' condition either skips all prerequisites or is undefined for partial success.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `contracts.register_contract` is idempotent for identical key+model pairs (or add a guard in `passes.register_contracts()`) so that re-importing or reloading the module does not raise or silently corrupt the registry.
evidence: Plan adds a new registration call to an existing shared-registry function but says nothing about idempotency of re-registration.
Test suite reloads modules between test runs; if registration raises on duplicate key, tests will fail non-deterministically.
findings.finding: The `contracts.register_contract(...)` call in `passes.register_contracts()` mutates a shared contract registry; the plan does not state whether re-registering the same contract key is idempotent, which risks test-isolation failures or silent overwrites during module reload.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Add its contracts.register_contract("plan_review_prerequisite_coverage", _prerequisite_coverage_model) call to the existing passes.register_contracts() function'
scenarios: A test imports `passes` twice or pytest reloads the module; duplicate registration raises an error or silently overwrites the model, causing subtle schema mismatches.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a guard such as `if 'plan_review_prerequisite_coverage' not in contracts.registry: contracts.register_contract(...)` or document that `register_contract` is a no-op for duplicate identical registrations.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a motivation section that reproduces or concretely names the current behavior gap: e.g., cite a real or synthetic case where an existing plan was inconsistent with a prerequisite and the current review process failed to catch it, or state explicitly what system or user problem arises from the absence of this check.
evidence: 'Evaluate whether a ticket plan remains consistent with each direct prerequisite' — states a goal but not a problem the current system causes
No section names a concrete case where the existing review passed a plan that contradicted a prerequisite
The upstream contract references cite interface compatibility (certified material-pin and review-phase interfaces) but not a demonstrated failure mode of the current system
ABSENCE: no reproduction steps, no Expected vs. Actual, no named user or system problem the current behavior causes
findings.finding: The plan proposes a significant new mechanism (prerequisite consistency review pass, new contracts, batching logic, schema extensions, orchestrator changes) without establishing that the current behavior is wrong or insufficient — no reproduction of a missed inconsistency, no Expected/Actual, no named defect or gap the current reviewer causes by lacking this check.
findings.location: Goal and Upstream contract sections
scenarios: The mechanism is built but addresses a gap that the current system either already handles through other means or that rarely occurs in practice, resulting in wasted complexity
A reviewer asks 'why was this added?' and the answer is 'it seemed like a good idea' rather than pointing to a concrete failure
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Motivation' subsection under Goal: describe one concrete scenario (real or illustrative) where a plan's implementation approach contradicted a direct prerequisite's contract, the current review missed it, and the downstream consequence. Alternatively, cite a named defect or epic requirement that mandates this check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `_build_verification_output` can accept an extra-binary-fields argument and inject `prerequisite_attribution_valid` into the Binary model *without* modifying `_build_binary`'s signature or loop, or explicitly name the injection mechanism (e.g., post-construction field addition via `create_model` override).
evidence: verify_models.py:91: `def _build_binary(forbid: Any) -> type:` — all binary fields constructed internally, no injection hook
verify_models.py:115: `Binary = _build_binary(forbid)` called inside `_build_verification_output` with no extra-fields param
Plan text: 'Do not add the field to shared `_build_binary`, `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT`, or `GRADED_BINARY`'
Plan text: 'Extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument'
verify_models.py:102-104: `for q in GRADED_BINARY: ... binary_fields[q] = ...` — the field loop is inside _build_binary, not accessible from _build_verification_output's caller
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument and have only `plan_review_verification_model` pass `prerequisite_attribution_valid` with default `na`... Do not add the field to shared `_build_binary`, `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT`, or `GRADED_BINARY`.' Reading `verify_models.py:91-105`, `_build_binary(forbid)` constructs ALL binary fields internally with no injection hook, and is called inside `_build_verification_output(forbid, severity_cls)` (line 115) with no way to inject extra fields from outside. Any 'extra-binary-fields' argument added to `_build_verification_output` would need to thread into `_build_binary`'s construction loop — effectively modifying `_build_binary`'s call interface even if not its signature. The plan's claim that this extension is achievable 'without touching `_build_binary`' is an unverified implementation-mechanism assertion about the actual kernel architecture.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph: 'Reuse the registered index-keyed `plan_review_verification` contract for focused Pass 2.'
scenarios: Implementor adds extra_binary_fields param to _build_verification_output but has no clean way to pass it into _build_binary without touching _build_binary — forces a choice the plan explicitly forbids.
Alternative: post-construction model patching (after _build_verification_output returns) — fragile and not described.
If _build_binary is modified to accept extra fields, its call sites in all three verification models must be audited, contradicting the plan's isolation claim.
findings.suggested_fix: Name the concrete injection mechanism: e.g., 'after calling `_build_binary(forbid)`, create a patched Binary submodel via `create_model("Binary", __base__=Binary, prerequisite_attribution_valid=(str, Field(default="na", description="yes|no|na")))` inside `_build_verification_output` when `extra_binary_fields` is non-empty.' This makes the plan verifiable against the actual kernel architecture.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/workflow/test_plan_review_verify_recovery.py` and `tests/unit/workflow/test_plan_review_assemble_memo.py` to the Verification section (or explicitly note they are unaffected because `coverage` defaults to no `prerequisite_indeterminate` key).
evidence: Grep shows `tests/unit/workflow/test_plan_review_verify_recovery.py` calls `gate_dispatch._recover_plan_review_verify_failure` (line 242) which in turn calls `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` (workflow_ops.py:557).
Grep shows `tests/unit/workflow/test_plan_review_assemble_memo.py` calls `gate_dispatch.produce_plan_review_verdict` (line 115), which also reaches `finalize_verdict`.

The plan's Verification section only names `tests/unit/test_plan_review.py`, `tests/unit/test_plan_review_prerequisites.py`, `tests/unit/workflow/test_plan_review_workflow.py`, `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_plan_review_prerequisite_isolation.py`, `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_gate.py`.
findings.finding: The plan modifies `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` to add an `if coverage.get('prerequisite_indeterminate'): verdict = 'INDETERMINATE'` branch, but does not enumerate the external test consumers of this function that will be affected: `tests/unit/workflow/test_plan_review_verify_recovery.py` (calls `finalize_verdict` directly and calls `gate_dispatch._recover_plan_review_verify_failure` which invokes it) and `tests/unit/workflow/test_plan_review_assemble_memo.py` (calls `produce_plan_review_verdict`).
findings.location: Implementation section: 'This story explicitly modifies `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`...'
scenarios: After adding the indeterminate-prerequisite branch to finalize_verdict, the existing verify-recovery tests start failing because they assert specific verdict strings from finalize_verdict without supplying `prerequisite_indeterminate` in coverage, and the test suite is not in the plan's verification scope.
findings.suggested_fix: Either add these test files to the `pytest` command in Verification, or add a sentence noting that `coverage.get('prerequisite_indeterminate')` defaults falsy when absent, so existing callers that never set it are unaffected.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for routing the prerequisite-only finder through `ProductionBatchRunner` rather than as a registered `uses` op in `workflow_ops.py`; if the workflow-op pattern is equally feasible, consider adopting it for codebase-alignment and stronger schema-boundary isolation.
evidence: Plan text: 'Extend the production Pass-1 batch runner with the prerequisite-only finder route. It receives the subject plan plus stable whole prerequisite blocks and invokes `plan_review_prerequisite_coverage`.'

All existing gate capabilities (precheck, assemble_criteria, verify_inputs, coach_inputs, decide, coach, passthrough) are registered ops in `workflow_ops.py` and wired as steps in `plan-review.yaml`; `ProductionBatchRunner` is a thin adapter for the finder batch only, not a routing hub for distinct pass types.

The workflow YAML pattern provides isolation guarantees at the schema boundary (input/output schemas), making the contract between steps contractually enforced rather than programmatically enforced inside the runner.
No rationale is given for why this approach was chosen over adding a dedicated `plan_review_prerequisite_finder` op + corresponding YAML step.
findings.finding: The plan routes the prerequisite-only finder through `ProductionBatchRunner` rather than as a registered `uses` op in `workflow_ops.py` wired into `plan-review.yaml`, which is the established architectural pattern for every other gate capability. No positive rationale is given for this divergence.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Extend the production Pass-1 batch runner with the prerequisite-only finder route.'
scenarios: The `ProductionBatchRunner` was designed as a thin finder adapter (D1–D5 in production_batch_runner.py docstring); routing a second pass type through it adds conditional branching that the runner was not designed to host, increasing coupling.
Isolation tests (`test_plan_review_prerequisite_isolation.py`) would need to intercept batch-runner internals rather than op boundaries, making them more brittle.
A future refactor of the batch runner seam must now carry knowledge of the prerequisite route.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document why two non-overlapping index domains (general + focused) are chosen over a single unified index with `prerequisite_id` as a discriminator, or consider adopting the unified approach if no strong reason exists.
evidence: 'Preserve two non-overlapping verification index domains. General arrays retain 0..G-1. Flatten focused findings in stable (prerequisite_id, finding_index) order into local 0..P-1 and verify only that list.'

No mention of considering a single unified index domain with prerequisite_id as a discriminator — an established pattern in multi-source review pipelines — or why independent reshaping is preferable to a single pass.
Absence of any prior-art or alternative-considered note for this architectural split.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a custom two-domain index architecture (general 0..G-1 and focused 0..P-1 with independent reshaping, two separate decide calls, and post-hoc concatenation) without justifying this design over a simpler unified index with a discriminator field. No prior art or trade-off analysis is cited.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Preserve two non-overlapping verification index domains'
scenarios: The dual-domain design requires two separate decide calls, two reshape passes, and a post-hoc concatenation step; each join point is a new bug surface. If the unified-index alternative was considered and rejected, the reason should be documented so future maintainers can safely evolve the design.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `models_at_or_above()` (or its intended replacement) supports context-window-ranked escalation for the singleton overflow case, and cite or define its interface in the plan.
evidence: 'Escalate a singleton through models_at_or_above().'
No pointer to where models_at_or_above is defined, what its signature is, or whether it already handles context-window escalation for this use case.
Unverified capability assertion: the function may exist for a different purpose (e.g., capability gating) rather than window-size escalation.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `models_at_or_above()` exists and supports escalation to larger-window models on context-limit singleton failure, but this capability is unverified within the plan and no reference to its definition or interface contract is provided.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Size the actual focused prompt topology … Escalate a singleton through models_at_or_above()'
scenarios: If models_at_or_above() does not accept or return models ranked by context window size, the singleton escalation path silently fails and the implementation falls through to evaluation-error without attempting a larger model.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the `contracts` registry supports optional extra fields additively (i.e., `plan_review_verification_model` outputs with `prerequisite_attribution_valid` validate correctly, and outputs without it also validate correctly), and document this behavior in the plan.
evidence: 'Reuse the registered index-keyed plan_review_verification contract for focused Pass 2. Extend _build_verification_output with an optional extra-binary-fields argument and have only plan_review_verification_model pass prerequisite_attribution_valid with default na.'
'verification_model and code_review_verification_model pass no extra field and remain schema-identical.'
Whether the contract/schema validation system treats unknown or optional extra fields as valid (additive) or as a schema violation is not established in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the existing `plan_review_verification` contract (registered as index-keyed) can be extended with an optional `prerequisite_attribution_valid` field via `_build_verification_output`'s extra-binary-fields argument without breaking existing consumers or schema validation, but no verification of the contract system's optional-field semantics is provided.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Reuse the registered index-keyed plan_review_verification contract for focused Pass 2'
scenarios: If the contract registry performs strict schema validation, adding an optional field to one caller's output but not others could cause validation failures at runtime, or silently accept malformed outputs from other callers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether this story adds `execution_review` to `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings` (extending 5967's interface early) or depends on 5967 landing first, and state this as an explicit prerequisite ordering constraint.
evidence: Plan says: 'Determine execution_review once from the review-phase snapshot supplied by 5967 and pass the same boolean to both general and focused pass3_over_findings(..., execution_review=execution_review) calls'
Existing orchestrator.pass3_over_findings (orchestrator.py:678-703) signature: (findings, verifs) — no execution_review parameter
Existing kernel pass3_over_findings (decide.py:579-606) signature: (findings, verifs, *, threshold_for, impact_fn=None) — no execution_review parameter
Ticket 5967's description defines orchestrator.pass3_over_findings with execution_review: bool = False, but 5967 is open/unimplemented at time of this plan
findings.finding: The plan instructs passing `execution_review=execution_review` to both general and focused `pass3_over_findings(...)` calls, but the existing `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings` wrapper (confirmed in code) takes only `(findings, verifs)` with no `execution_review` parameter, and the kernel `pass3_over_findings` also has no such parameter. This parameter is defined as 5967's deliverable. The plan states 'Determine execution_review once from the review-phase snapshot supplied by 5967' but never specifies whether this story adds the execution_review parameter to orchestrator.pass3_over_findings itself, or whether it relies on 5967 having already done so. Since both stories are open, the interface this plan calls is not yet defined.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'pass the same boolean to both general and focused pass3_over_findings(..., execution_review=execution_review) calls'
scenarios: This story ships without 5967 complete; orchestrator.pass3_over_findings does not accept execution_review, causing a TypeError at runtime when the focused path calls it
The implementor adds execution_review to orchestrator.pass3_over_findings in this story, duplicating 5967's work and creating a merge conflict

findings.finding: The Verification section says 'Run the new opt-in prerequisite packing evaluation and record its result in a ticket comment' but never names the concrete command or script to invoke it. An executor cannot satisfy AC item 13 without knowing what to run.
findings.location: Verification section, bullet 3 / AC item 13
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fourth bullet to Verification: 'Run `.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/parity/test_prerequisite_packing_eval.py --prerequisite-parity` (or the equivalent entry point defined during implementation) and paste the printed recall/false-acceptance/attribution summary as a ticket comment.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'per the epic contract, `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` tests this flag [coverage.prerequisite_indeterminate] before existing verdict branches and forces top-level `INDETERMINATE`' — but reading `orchestrator.py:497` confirms `finalize_verdict` currently checks only `coverage.get('llm_unavailable')` and `coverage.get('verify_failed')`. No `prerequisite_indeterminate` check exists. The phrase 'per the epic contract' implies this behavior is already established upstream, but there is no evidence in the codebase of any such contract, and the plan does not reference a specific story or ticket that already implemented it.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Wire focused finder and verifier steps and all focused arrays through both agentic and single-turn branches in `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml`. Thread final coverage into `plan_review_coach`, which sets `coverage.prerequisite_indeterminate=true` when any record is indeterminate. Per the epic contract, `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` tests this flag before existing verdict branches and forces top-level `INDETERMINATE`'

findings.finding: The plan says `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` tests the `coverage.prerequisite_indeterminate` flag and forces top-level INDETERMINATE before existing verdict branches, but `finalize_verdict` is not listed as a named edit target and the plan does not describe what behavioral change is made to it.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'orchestrator.finalize_verdict tests this flag before existing verdict branches and forces top-level INDETERMINATE'

findings.finding: The plan says `plan_review_coach` 'sets `coverage.prerequisite_indeterminate=true` when any record is indeterminate', and that '`orchestrator.finalize_verdict` tests this flag before existing verdict branches and forces top-level `INDETERMINATE`'. However, the data flow path from the coach step's coverage mutation to `finalize_verdict`'s `coverage` parameter is never specified. In the existing YAML workflow, `finalize_verdict` is called inside `plan_review_coach` (the `uses` op), which receives coverage from the verdict assembly chain. The plan does not specify: (a) whether `plan_review_coach` merges `prerequisite_indeterminate` into the coverage dict it passes to `finalize_verdict`, (b) whether `finalize_verdict` receives this as a new parameter, or (c) whether the YAML workflow threads a new `prerequisite_indeterminate` field from a prior step.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph: 'Wire focused finder and verifier steps and all focused arrays through both agentic and single-turn branches in `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml`. Thread final coverage into `plan_review_coach`, which sets `coverage.prerequisite_indeterminate=true`… `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` tests this flag before existing verdict branches.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Extend its shared `Binary` with optional `prerequisite_attribution_valid=yes|no|na`; include it in `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT`, and exclude it from `GRADED_BINARY`' — but reading verify_models.py confirms `_build_binary()` generates ONE Binary type shared by both `plan_review_verification_model` AND `code_review_verification_model`. Adding `prerequisite_attribution_valid` to `_build_binary`/`_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT` will inject the field into code-review's Pass-2 output schema and sidecar as well. The plan neither acknowledges this cross-gate field injection nor specifies whether the field is added only to a plan-review-specific Binary subtype or to the shared one — the phrase 'its shared Binary' is ambiguous but implies the shared builder.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Extend its shared `Binary` with optional `prerequisite_attribution_valid=yes|no|na`; default absent values to `na`, include it in `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT`, and exclude it from `GRADED_BINARY`'
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the existing pattern for plan-review-specific extensions: define `prerequisite_attribution_valid` only inside `plan_review_verification_model`'s local `PlanSeverityAttrs` or a plan-review-specific Binary subtype, not in the shared `_build_binary()`. Update `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT` with a conditional or add it only to the plan-review-specific factory.

findings.finding: The plan says to 'Extend its shared Binary with optional prerequisite_attribution_valid=yes|no|na' but the shared `_build_binary()` in `rebar.llm.review_kernel.verify_models` is used by BOTH `verification_model` (code-review) and `plan_review_verification_model` (plan-review). Adding a plan-review-prerequisite-specific veto field to the shared builder leaks plan-review semantics into the code-review Binary, appearing silently in code-review verifications with no meaning.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Use the parent-approved existing index-keyed plan_review_verification schema for focused Pass 2 rather than adding another verification contract. Extend its shared Binary with optional prerequisite_attribution_valid=yes|no|na'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `prerequisite_attribution_valid` only in the `plan_review_verification_model` factory (alongside the plan-severity axes that are already plan-review-specific there), not in the shared `_build_binary()` that the base `verification_model` uses.

findings.finding: The plan's language 'forces top-level unsigned INDETERMINATE' before 'existing verdict branches' is ambiguous about ordering relative to a DET block: if `coverage.prerequisite_indeterminate=true` is tested before `if blocking:` in `finalize_verdict`, a DET-blocked plan with an indeterminate prerequisite would yield INDETERMINATE instead of BLOCK, hiding a real blocking defect.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'per the epic contract, `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` tests this flag before existing verdict branches and forces top-level `INDETERMINATE`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'before existing verdict branches' with 'after the DET-block branch and before the final PASS branch': i.e., insert the prerequisite_indeterminate check after `elif indeterminate and not surfaced: verdict='INDETERMINATE'` and before `else: verdict='PASS'`, so a DET block still yields BLOCK.

findings.finding: The plan references 'the phase story threshold function' without defining what it is in the focused-domain context. The phrase appears in 'Run `pass3_over_findings` on remaining focused findings with focused verifications and the phase story threshold function.' Story 5967 (the certified upstream dependency) defines `pass3_over_findings(findings, verifs, *, execution_review: bool = False)` with a floor-raising wrapper, but the plan never specifies whether the focused prerequisite domain uses the execution-review variant or a plain threshold function, nor where the `execution_review` flag for the focused call comes from.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph: 'In `plan_review_decide`, independently reshape and decide each domain… Run `pass3_over_findings` on remaining focused findings with focused verifications and the phase story threshold function.'

findings.finding: The plan refers to 'stable-order whole prerequisite blocks' and 'Flatten focused findings separately in stable `(prerequisite_id, finding_index)` order into local domain 0..P-1', but never defines what 'stable order' means for prerequisite IDs. The prerequisite IDs are canonical ticket IDs (UUIDs like `75de-02b0-d454-410b`), but the ordering criterion (lexicographic, insertion order from `PlanRelationSnapshot`, or another deterministic key) is unspecified. The packing evaluation specifically requires 'tail-conflict with the conflict last in stable order', implying order is semantically meaningful — a conflict in the last bin must be evaluated identically to a singleton call — but the order definition needed to write gold fixtures is absent.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraphs: 'packs as many blocks as fit…stable-order whole prerequisite blocks' and 'Flatten focused findings separately in stable `(prerequisite_id, finding_index)` order into local domain 0..P-1'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Stable order is ascending lexicographic order of the canonical ticket ID string. All prerequisite-to-block packing and the focused local domain flattening use this order.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py` to the plan's edit-target list, since `_build_verification_output` lives there and must be extended with the optional extra-binary-fields argument.
evidence: Grep confirms `_build_verification_output` is in `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py` (line 108) — outside the plan-review dir.
`src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py` is absent from the plan's file list and from the Verification section.
The plan names `verification_model` and `code_review_verification_model` as 'pass no extra field and remain schema-identical', requiring those functions in the same file to be inspected/touched.
findings.finding: `_build_verification_output` lives in `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py`, which is outside the plan-review directory. The plan says 'extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument and have only `plan_review_verification_model` pass `prerequisite_attribution_valid`', but `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py` is not listed anywhere as an edit target.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Reuse the registered index-keyed `plan_review_verification` contract for focused Pass 2. Extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument'
scenarios: Implementer adds the extra-binary-fields arg to `_build_verification_output` but the review_kernel module is not in scope for testing or sign-off, causing a missed change.
A future change to `_build_verification_output` for another feature inadvertently clobbers this extension.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py` to the Implementation section's file/symbol list and to the Verification section's pytest scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name `src/rebar/llm/parity.py` as an edit target for adding `gold_prerequisite_id`/`pred_prerequisite_id` to `ItemRecord` and for defining `prerequisite_fidelity_report`, and verify existing `container_fidelity_report`/`parity_report` consumers are unaffected.
evidence: Grep confirms `class ItemRecord` is defined exclusively in `src/rebar/llm/parity.py` (line 41).
`src/rebar/llm/parity.py` does not appear in the plan text as a file to modify.
`fidelity_spot_eval.py` imports `from rebar.llm.parity import ItemRecord` — meaning any field addition to ItemRecord also affects that import site.
findings.finding: The plan adds `gold_prerequisite_id` and `pred_prerequisite_id` fields to `parity.ItemRecord` and adds `prerequisite_fidelity_report`, but `src/rebar/llm/parity.py` — the only file that defines `ItemRecord` and where `prerequisite_fidelity_report` would live — is not named anywhere as an edit target.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Add optional `gold_prerequisite_id` and `pred_prerequisite_id` fields to `parity.ItemRecord`... Add `prerequisite_fidelity_report`'
scenarios: Implementer modifies `parity.py` without it being in scope, risking missing the associated attestation/review requirement for that file.
`container_fidelity_report` and `parity_report` in `parity.py` may also need changes to handle the new prerequisite fields — absent from the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/llm/parity.py` to the Implementation file list with the rationale 'Extend ItemRecord with opt-in prerequisite attribution fields and define prerequisite_fidelity_report'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the `|prereq:<canonical-id>` string-append approach over structured identity (e.g., a `(text_hash, prerequisite_id)` tuple hash), and confirm the separator cannot appear in finding text or canonical IDs to rule out collision.
evidence: 'Extend mint_finding_id so its hash basis appends |prereq:<canonical-id> when prerequisite_id is present; identical text from different prerequisites cannot deduplicate each other.'
The plan does not mention whether structured finding identity (prerequisite_id as a first-class field in the identity tuple) was considered as an alternative to string-concatenation hashing.
String concatenation in hash bases is known to create collision edge cases (e.g., a finding text that itself contains the substring '|prereq:').
findings.finding: The plan introduces a custom hash-extension approach for finding-ID deduplication (`mint_finding_id` appends `|prereq:<canonical-id>`) without considering whether the existing finding-ID scheme already accommodates a structured identity field, or whether adding a prerequisite_id to the Finding struct itself (prior art in structured identity) would be less fragile.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Extend mint_finding_id so its hash basis appends |prereq:<canonical-id> when prerequisite_id is present'
scenarios: A finding whose text happens to contain '|prereq:<some-id>' could collide with or be confused for a prerequisite-scoped finding from a different prerequisite, undermining the deduplication guarantee the plan relies on.
findings.suggested_fix: Use a two-field hash input such as `hashlib.sha256(finding_text.encode() + b'\x00' + canonical_id.encode())` with a null-byte separator that cannot appear in either field, rather than a printable-character separator.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
